title: Skill improves accuracy in the identifiable benchmark
claim: Blended skill + stake weighting reduces forecast error relative to equal weights when the DGP has a latent truth.
interpretation: The online skill layer tracks intrinsic forecaster quality well in the latent-truth setting, so up-weighting better forecasters improves the aggregate.
caveat: This is a clean benchmark with identifiable truth, not a strategic or adversarial setting.

title: Skill estimates recover after perturbation
claim: The EWMA skill layer recovers correct ordering within ~50 rounds after a shock to all skill weights.
interpretation: The exponential decay parameter γ controls the speed-accuracy trade-off: faster decay recovers quicker but is noisier.
caveat: Recovery speed depends on γ, signal-to-noise ratio, and the number of active agents.

title: Wealth concentration stays controlled
claim: Gini and HHI do not rise sharply under skill + stake weighting compared to equal weights.
interpretation: Skill gating attenuates noisy large bets without concentrating wealth in few agents.
caveat: Under some deposit policies (e.g. wealth-fraction), concentration can still rise if skilled agents compound returns.

title: Calibration holds in the centre, weakens in the tails
claim: The aggregate forecast is well-calibrated for central quantiles but PIT coverage degrades for extreme quantiles.
metric: PIT coverage gap < 5% for central, > 10% for tails
interpretation: The linear pool preserves calibration for moderate probability events but under-disperses in the tails.
caveat: Tail miscalibration is the main modelling limitation and is shared across all weighting methods.

title: Equal weighting is a strong baseline
claim: Uniform weights are surprisingly hard to beat, especially under non-stationarity or small panels.
interpretation: Skill + stake helps most when the panel includes both strong and weak forecasters and enough rounds for skill to converge.
caveat: With few agents or short horizons, estimation noise in σ can erase the advantage.
